Referring to future actions and events; Expressing politeness; Other: Expressing intent
comment:
'Expressing politeness' is marked because 'shall' sounds softer and more polite than 'will', especially in questions (e.g., "Shall we go?"). In the English I speak I rarely use 'shall' because it sounds a bit archaic and comically formal, but there are some varieties of English where it's used more regularly because it sounds less direct than 'will'.

I was going to also add 'expressing obligation' because in some contexts, 'shall' implies that the speaker is bound to do whatever they're saying (e.g., "Our institution shall respect everyone's opinons." = "Our institution promises to respect everyone's opinions.") but that's probably more of a C1 communicative function rather than A2.

functions: Other: Comparing within a set
comment: This construct points out a unique entity within a set based on the characteristic described by the adjective: e.g., out of all mobile phones in the shop, this one stands out based on its price.

functions: Comparing within a set; Comparing
comment: Similarly to the "the + ADJ.SUP" construct, this construct compares entities within a set but relatively rather than by singling out one unique entity like the comparative construction.

functions: Other: Referring to past actions and events
comment:
"I have left my suitcase in your house" is the most straightforward of the examples because it refers to the action of leaving the suitcase being completed in the past.
The second example refers to the duration of time that has passed since some time in the past, rather than a completed action, because of the use of "been".
The third example is a bit confusing because "have you got" is usually just a synonym for "have", but if we take the same form with a different word we also get a construction referring to the past, e.g., "Have you done your homework?". I guess you could also say that "have you got" implies the getting of the mobile phone at some point in the past?

comment: Verb + Preposition is really quite tricky because of phrasal verbs in English. "Get up you lazybones!"  uses the same structure as "Please get up this ladder and look for a hole in the roof", but the phrasal verb has a special meaning (to get out of bed). So when the sentence is deconstructed like this, the meanings can be vastly different depending on the way the preposition is used. For example, 'look after' in the example text here is really a phrasal verb - after isn't being used as a preposition of position or order (but you could make a sentence where it is being used that way ("Sometimes people drop coins during the race, you should look after they've run through here.")). I don't know what you can do about this, but it is something I guess you should at least be aware of.

functions: Referring to present states, events and facts; Other: Giving instructions
comment: One difficult thing here is that I am choosing 'Referring to present states, events and facts' both here and for the Present Continuous before. It is true for both of them, but in different ways - present simple is for things that are usually or always true, while present continuous is for things that are true right now but not always. Both of these could be 'referring to present states', but I don't know if conflating them under one function is appropriate.

functions: Expressing negation; Referring to present states, events and facts; Referring to the acting or experiencing entity; Referring to third-person subjects
comment:
What is the difference between a 'third-person subject' and 'the acting or experiencing entity' - both appear to describe the subject of the sentence, with the only different being that 'third-person' rules out I, you, or we. And given that 'SUBJ' is part of the construction here, it seems obvious that the construction 'refers to' a subject.  Honestly, very few constructions (other than the passive be + PP) DON'T refer to a subject. Maybe that category is too broad? Or maybe I simply am not properly understanding the category.
I also don't understand why the options presented here for present simple negative aren't the same as those for present simple, but with the addition of 'expressing negation'. These two constructions are so similar, that their functions must also be similar.
I don't know, I'm finding this exercise quite difficult!

Other: Referring to past actions and events
comment:
"I have left my suitcase in your house" is the most straightforward of the examples because it refers to the action of leaving the suitcase being completed in the past.
The second example refers to the duration of time that has passed since some time in the past, rather than a completed action, because of the use of "been".
The third example is a bit confusing because "have you got" is usually just a synonym for "have", but if we take the same form with a different word we also get a construction referring to the past, e.g., "Have you done your homework?". I guess you could also say that "have you got" implies the getting of the mobile phone at some point in the past?

comment:
'Expressing politeness' is marked because 'shall' sounds softer and more polite than 'will', especially in questions (e.g., "Shall we go?"). In the English I speak I rarely use 'shall' because it sounds a bit archaic and comically formal, but there are some varieties of English where it's used more regularly because it sounds less direct than 'will'.

I was going to also add 'expressing obligation' because in some contexts, 'shall' implies that the speaker is bound to do whatever they're saying (e.g., "Our institution shall respect everyone's opinons." = "Our institution promises to respect everyone's opinions.") but that's probably more of a C1 communicative function rather than A2.
